title: Alfajores
subtitle: South American dulce de leche sandwich biscuits
excerpt: Sixteen soft, crumbly South American sandwich biscuits filled with dulce de leche. The dough uses cornflour as well as flour, which gives a melt-in-the-mouth, almost powdery texture. Rolled in desiccated coconut or dusted with icing sugar to finish.
sourceNotes: Alfajores are documented in South American cookery, deriving from a medieval Andalusian sweet (alajú) brought to the Americas through Spanish colonisation. Recipes appear in pre-1928 Argentine and Peruvian household cookery texts; the dulce de leche-filled version is documented in pre-1928 Argentine domestic science publications and in early-twentieth-century Buenos Aires newspaper cookery columns available through Argentine national library archives.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Biscuits keep dry for 3 days. Fill within 2 hours of serving for best texture, or up to a day ahead if you prefer a soft biscuit.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 175°C. Alfajores should be pale, not golden. They are very fragile when hot; leave on the tray to firm up.
baking.decoratingTechnique: roll the edges in desiccated coconut or dust tops with icing sugar

glossaryTerms.definition: Replacing some of the plain flour with cornflour in a biscuit dough produces a shorter, more crumbly texture. Cornflour contains no gluten; reducing the overall gluten content of the dough prevents it from becoming tough or chewy. The result is a biscuit that melts rather than snaps. Alfajores use a higher than usual proportion of cornflour specifically to achieve this soft, crumbly quality: the biscuit should dissolve on the tongue, not require chewing.

content.text: Alfajores are South American sandwich biscuits: two crumbly discs filled with dulce de leche. The dough uses a high proportion of
to produce the characteristic melt-in-the-mouth crumb. Sixteen biscuits. The dough needs 30 minutes in the fridge before rolling.

Beat the butter and icing sugar together until pale and creamy. Add the egg yolks and vanilla and beat until smooth. Sift in the plain flour, cornflour, baking powder, and salt and fold until a soft, smooth dough forms. Flatten into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 165°C fan (175°C conventional). Roll the chilled dough to 5 mm thick on a lightly floured surface. Cut out 32 rounds with a 5 cm cutter and place on lined baking trays. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes, until just set but still very pale. Leave on the trays for 10 minutes before moving; they are fragile when hot. Cool completely before filling.

Spread a generous teaspoon of dulce de leche on the flat side of half the biscuits, then press a second biscuit on top, flat side in. Dust the tops with icing sugar or roll the exposed filling edge in desiccated coconut if you like.
